title: 7 — Peter
content: I can't stop looking at it.

The photograph lies flat on my lap under the desk lamp, the woman at Bly's door caught mid-turn, coat belted tight, one hand raised as if about to knock—or about to wave someone away. Her face. Her face is three-quarters turned toward the camera, and the angle cuts her features into something almost familiar. Almost. The jawline. The way the collar rides up on the left side because the coat doesn't fit right at the shoulder. My mother wore her coats like that. One shoulder slightly lower than the other from years of carrying a bag on that side. You could see it in every photograph ever taken of her.
Or you could see it in me. Standing in a doorway. Raising one hand.

I pick up my phone. Open the camera. Frame the photograph on my desk—full frame, no shadow, no glare. The lamp repositions twice before I get it right. I take three shots. Then I flip the photograph over and photograph the back. Blank. No inscription, no date, no photographer's mark. Just the texture of old photo paper, yellowed where fingers have touched the edges over the years. Many fingers. Many years.

I hide it in the false bottom of my toiletry case, beneath a zippered lining I sewed myself the summer before I left for university. A hiding place inside a hiding place. Evidence. That's what this is now. Not a gift, not a welcome, not a message meant to comfort. Evidence that someone at Bly knew exactly who I was before I walked through that door. Evidence that the woman in the coat—my mother, or someone who looks enough like her to make my hands shake—was here. Was at this door.
I need to get into the tower room.

That thought has been circling since last night, since I found the Polaroid on my pillow, since I traced the words WELCOME HOME with my fingertip and felt the indent where someone had pressed too hard with the marker. The tower room. Peter's room. Everyone at Bly orbits around the absence of Peter Quint. His ghost sits in at meals. His ghost opens doors that should be locked. His ghost leaves photographs on pillows.
I need to see where he lived. I need to touch the walls he touched. I need to understand what kind of man inspires this much silence.

Breakfast is eggs and toast and the quiet scrape of Mrs. Grose's spatula against the skillet. She moves around the kitchen with the exhaustion of someone who has made this exact meal in this exact kitchen ten thousand times. The eggs are perfect. The toast is barely browned. Everything is correct and nothing is joyful.
She sets my plate down and returns to the counter, her back to me, hands working a dishrag over the already-clean surface.
"Mrs. Grose."
"Mm."
"Tell me about Peter Quint."
Her hands stop.

Not slow down. Not hesitate. Stop. The dishrag hangs from her fingers, dripping onto the counter. Her shoulders go rigid, the fabric of her blouse pulling tight across her back. I can see the tendons in her neck stand out like cables.

She turns. Her face has closed—it's the only word for it. Like a door shutting. Like a window slamming down. Every trace of the tired, competent woman who made my eggs is gone, and what's left is a wall with eyes.
"Breakfast is over," she says. Not to me. To the room. To the clock on the wall. To the eggs still steaming on my plate.
She takes her dishrag and walks out. The kitchen door swings behind her once, twice, settles.
Across the table, Miles smiles.

He's seated where I didn't see him sit, at the far end, a slice of toast in one hand, a book open beside his plate. Ten years old. Hair not yet combed. Still in his pajama top, the buttons misaligned by one. He looks like a child who has just been told a secret he finds delicious.
"You shouldn't ask Mrs. G about him," he says. "It upsets her."
"Why?"
He takes a bite of toast. Chews. Swallows. Taking his time. "Because she found him."
"Found him."
"At the bottom of the tower." He says it the way you'd say a city name. Baltimore. The bottom of the tower. A location, not a tragedy.
"He fell?"
Miles shrugs. The toast comes up for another bite. "People fall."

He returns to his book. The conversation, for him, is finished. I watch him read—actually read, his eyes tracking left to right, his lips slightly parted—and I think about what it means that a ten-year-old can say someone fell from a tower and shrug. What it means that he's already learned the most useful skill a person can have at Bly: the ability to treat catastrophe as weather. Something that happens. Something you walk through.
I eat my eggs. They've gone cold. I don't taste them.

The library at Bly is a long room on the east side of the house, and it smells exactly the way a library should—old paper, leather binding, the faint sourness of glue that has outlived its usefulness. The shelves go floor to ceiling, and half the books are where they should be, and the other half are stacked on tables and windowsills and the floor in piles that suggest someone was looking for something and never found it, or found it and didn't care about the mess they left behind.

I'm seated in the chair by the window—not reading, thinking—when Miles appears in the doorway. He stands there a moment, watching me, and there's something different in his posture. No performance. No calculations behind his eyes. He looks younger. Smaller. Like a boy who wants to talk.
"Can I come in?"
"It's not my library."

He crosses to the shelf nearest me and trails his fingers along the spines. Not looking at the books. Looking at his hand. "Peter used to read to us in here. Flora and me. He'd pick the oldest books—ones nobody had touched in years—and read them out loud like they were the most exciting stories ever written."
There it is again. Peter. His name in this house is a fingerprint on every surface.
"You liked him," I say. Not a question.
"I loved him." Miles says it simply. Factually. The way you'd say I have two hands. "Peter was our friend. He taught us things."
"What kinds of things?"
He stops walking. His hand rests on a book—something green and cracked, the spine lettering worn to ghosts. He doesn't look at me when he answers.

"How to see what other people can't." His voice is quieter now. Genuine in a way that makes my skin prickle, because his performances are alarming but his sincerity is worse. "How to be the most interesting person in any room. How to make people tell you their secrets."
"How to make people tell you their secrets," I repeat.
He turns. His eyes are clear. Open. A boy who loved his teacher and doesn't understand why that's supposed to be wrong.

"The trick," he says, "is that everyone wants to tell you. They're carrying their secrets around like—like stones in their pockets. They're heavy. And you just have to look like someone who won't drop them."
"Did Peter make you feel like that? Like you could tell him anything?"

"I didn't have anything to tell him." Miles picks up the green book, turns it over, sets it back down. "He already knew. That was the thing about Peter. He looked at you and he already knew what you were hiding. He'd say, 'You don't have to tell me, Miles. I can see it.' And he could. He actually could."
The leather of the chair creaks under my hands. I'm gripping the armrests. I make myself let go.
"Peter sounds like he was very perceptive."

"He was the most perceptive person I've ever met." Miles looks up, and for a moment—just a moment—he looks exactly like the boy in the admission file. The one who cried when his parents didn't come back. The one before Peter got to him and remade him into this: a child who can discuss secrets and falls and locked rooms without flinching. "He saw all of me. Even the parts I didn't want to see myself."
"That sounds frightening."
"It was." Miles smiles. Not his sharp, performative smile. Something softer. Something broken. "It was the most frightening thing that ever happened to me. And also the best."

He leaves. The library door clicks shut behind him, and the room fills with the silence of ten thousand unread pages, and I sit there, and I think about the mathematics of corruption. How it works. How a person like Peter Quint doesn't take a child and break him. That's the mistake everyone makes. They think corruption is violent. That it leaves marks. Peter didn't break Miles. Peter saw Miles—saw the cracks that were already there, the ones every child has—and he loved those cracks. He made Miles love them too. He taught Miles that his broken places were interesting, that they made him special, that the ability to see other people's secrets was a gift and not a violation.
How to make people tell you their secrets.
I am at Bly because of a secret. And someone at Bly knows what it is.

Night at Bly is a thickness. It pools in the corridors like something poured, something viscous, something that muffles footsteps and swallows the sound of doors. I carry no flashlight. My phone is in my pocket, camera ready, but I don't need it yet. The hallway windows admit enough moonlight to walk by—blue-white slats that mark the floor like measurements.

The tower room is at the end of the east corridor, past the linen closet, past the bathroom with the dripping tap that Mrs. Grose has promised to fix since I arrived. The door is oak, heavy, with a brass plate where a name might once have been mounted. The screw holes are empty. Someone removed the plate and didn't replace it. Someone erased Peter's name from his own door.

The door is locked. I expected this. I turn the knob both directions, push, pull, rattle it in its frame. Solid. The keyhole is old—brass, worn smooth by years of keys—and I kneel and put my eye to it.

The room is dark but for the window. Moonlight falls across the floor in a pale rectangle, and it's enough. Enough to see that the room is untouched. A chair faces the window—a wooden chair, straight-backed, angled so whoever sat in it could see the lake. The surface of the lake is silver under the moon. From the tower, you could watch the lake and see anyone approaching the house from the road. You could watch everything.
A desk. Under the window, positioned so the sitter could look up from their work and see the water. The surface is bare except for—
A photograph. In a silver frame.

The frame catches the moonlight. It's ornate—scrollwork along the edges, a pattern of leaves and small flowers pressed into the metal. I know this frame. I have held its twin. The photograph on my pillow, the woman in the coat, arrived in a frame exactly like this one. The same scrollwork. The same leaves. The same small flowers. A pair. His and hers. Peter's desk and my pillow. Connected by silver and moonlight.

I can't see the photograph itself. The angle is wrong, or the light is wrong, or the frame is angled away from the keyhole, and I can only see the frame, the silver, the leaves—twin leaves, twin frames, twin photographs. The woman at Bly's door and whatever image Peter kept on his desk. A pair. A matched set.
My eye presses against the cold brass of the keyhole. My breath fogs the metal. I blink and press closer, tilting my head, trying to find the angle that will show me the face in that frame, and—
A hand touches my shoulder.
I spin. The back of my head cracks against the door and my hand comes up—instinct, useless—and I'm on my feet in the corridor, my heart slamming so hard I can feel it in my teeth, and there is—
No one.

The corridor is empty. Doorways on either side, closed. Windows at the far end, moonlight still marking the floor. The dripping tap in the bathroom. Nothing else. No one. The hallway stretches in both directions, empty, silent, and there is nowhere a person could have gone in the half-second between the touch and my turn. Nowhere.
I press my hand to my shoulder. The fabric of my shirt is cool under my palm. There's no warmth where the hand touched. No impression. No proof.
I turn back to the tower door.
It's ajar.

Not open. Not wide. Just—unlocked. The latch disengaged, the door resting a quarter-inch away from the frame, a sliver of darker darkness visible in the gap. The musty air from inside breaches the corridor—stale and close and old, the smell of a room that hasn't been breathed in for years, and it reaches me like an exhalation. Like the room is exhaling into the corridor. Into my face.

I didn't open it. I was leaning against it—my skull still aches where it knocked the oak—and the door didn't move when I pulled away. It was locked when I knelt down. It was locked when I pressed my eye to the keyhole. The keyhole where I saw the silver frame. The frame that matches mine.

Someone unlocked it while I was looking through the keyhole. Or it was never locked at all, and the locked feel was—what? A trick of the mechanism? The door settling? Or someone holding it shut from the inside and letting go at exactly the right moment?

The gap between door and frame waits. Patient. The room breathes its old air into the corridor. The chair faces the window. The desk sits under the moon. The photograph in its silver frame holds its image to the glass, turned away, hiding whoever Peter kept on his desk. The same frame. The same scrollwork. The same small flowers.
Twin frames. A pair. Peter and the woman. The woman at the door. My mother at the door. Or someone wearing her coat.

Someone wants me to go in. Or someone wants me to know they know I'm looking. Either way, the door is open now. The forbidden zone has cracked. And I stand in the corridor with my heart still racing and my shoulder still feeling the shape of five fingers that weren't there, and I think: who is in the tower photograph? And what will happen to me if I see their face?
